summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – CHILDREN – CRITICAL INCIDENT LIST – Where mother of the child recently deceased – Where applicant maternal grandmother deposed that the father of the child was unknown and says nothing of paternal family – Where s 67ZBD material indicated this was not so – Where s 67ZBD material indicates child sees father and paternal family alternate weekends – Where importance of the information sharing regime and co‑located officers highlighted.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BRASCH J:

The applicant in this matter is Ms Melton born in 1969 (“the maternal grandmother”). She is the maternal grandmother of the child X, who was born in 2021 (“the child”).
The child’s mother died in early 2026. The mother was Ms B and was described as having been the child’s primary carer.
The maternal grandmother described herself as a secondary carer, up to that point, and was actively involved with X’s care since birth. The mother lived with the maternal grandmother from the time of her pregnancy, and then upon the birth of the child, until about October 2025. The mother and child then moved to their own apartment. The maternal grandmother deposed that she continued with daily assistance and care.
In early 2026, the New South Wales Police Force attended upon the applicant’s home and advised her that her daughter had passed away. I have a letter from the Coroner’s Court before me with respect to the event.
Since the mother’s untimely death, the maternal grandmother deposed that the child has lived with her.
Quite rightly, the maternal grandmother identified that the child needed immediate stability, care and consistent living arrangements. Thus, the child had been living with the maternal grandmother in her home where she continues to support him.

What the applicant deposed to about the child’s father

The applicant deposed that she applied for this matter to come into the Critical Incident List because, on the mother’s passing, the child was left without a legal parent able to exercise parental responsibility. She said:[1]
...without interim orders, I am unable to make formal decisions regarding his education, medical or dental treatment, or to deal with Centrelink, Medicare, or other agencies on his behalf.
Interestingly, the maternal grandmother also said this:[2]
...His birth certificate does not record the name of a father, and no person has ever been legally recognised as his father or recorded as a parent, guardian, or person with parental responsibility.
That is correct. The child’s birth certificate does not have a father listed, but that is not the end of the matter.
The maternal grandmother then deposed:[3]
During her lifetime, my daughter, [Ms B], informed me that a particular man was [X’s] biological father. My knowledge of this is based solely on what my daughter told me. I have no independent evidence confirming paternity.
To my knowledge, the alleged father has never sought to be named on the child’s birth certificate or taken any steps to be legally recognised as [X’s] parent.
The maternal grandmother asked for the matter to be included in the Critical Incident List, and she further added that:[4]
I am not aware of the biological father being named or recorded on any official documentation as a parent, legal guardian, custodian, or next of kin of the child, including but not limited to medical, educational, or governmental record.
I am committed to supporting [X’s] relationship with his father should he present himself, where it is safe and appropriate

Material and information sharing

The body of the maternal grandmother’s affidavit, filed 26 February 2026, was witnessed by one person, but the annexures to her affidavit were witnessed by a different person. On this first return, I will not be distracted by a triumph of form over substance although I raised this with her new solicitor.
I also have before me:
(a) A letter from the Coroner’s Court;

(b) The mother’s birth certificate; and

(c) The child’s birth certificate, that lists the mother and no father to which I have already alluded.

The annexures to the maternal grandmother’s affidavit are marked as Exhibit 1.
As is my practice in the Critical Incident List, I sought reports pursuant to s 67ZBD of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) from the co-located officers of both the Department of Communities and Justice (NSW) (“the Department”) and the New South Wales Police Force (“the Police”) with respect to the child and the maternal grandmother.
I always do so in Critical Incident List matters because I often do not have a natural contradictor. The information sharing regime in the Act, therefore, is a way for me to see if child protection or police authorities have had eyes on the child, parents, or, in this case, the maternal grandmother.
Exhibit 3, which is the report received from the Police, has nothing of any child protection concerns whatsoever. It contains largely historical matters and primarily with the maternal grandmother as either a witness or a victim.

There is a father

The same cannot be said for Exhibit 2, which is a report from the Department.
In that s 67ZBD report, the child’s “household parent/carer 1” is described as follows:
(a) The child;

(b) A child called C, aged 6;

(c) Another child whose name is unknown, aged 10;

(d) Another child by the name of D, who is 14; and

(e) The maternal grandmother is listed as the “household parent/carers 1”.

The only impression that could be obtained from the maternal grandmother's affidavit was that she was not aware of the child’s father. However, Exhibit 2 goes on to describe “household parent/carers 2” and there it says:
Relation to child: Father of [X]; Maternal Uncle of [C] + Unknown (10)

Name: [Mr Edwards]

(emphasis added)
It then lists another person by the name of Ms E, being the paternal aunt of X, mother of C, the 10-year-old child, and D.
It also lists the paternal grandmother of X, and the maternal grandmother of C, by the name of Ms F, and, a maternal cousin by the name of G.
None of that information can be gleaned from the maternal grandmother’s affidavit.

Child safety involvement and more information

More importantly, the report [Exhibit 2] goes on to say that X, the subject child in these proceedings, had said he had “been [allegedly] sexually abused by his cousin” who was six, whilst at the paternal grandmother’s home when he was there over a [recent] weekend.
It goes on to say:
[X’s] dad [Mr Edwards], his paternal aunt [Ms E] and her children [C] and Unknown (10), in addition to possible other children/siblings, also reportedly live in [Ms F’s] home, where he visits every weekend, possibly every second weekend.
The material continues:
[X] went into the primary care of his maternal grandmother [Ms Melton] since Monday, after his mum [Ms B] unexpectedly passed away sometime over the weekend in their home ...
It adds:
(no formal arrangements made – [Mr Edwards] [the father] is fine w/this).

(Emphasis added)
I read parts of Exhibit 2 to the maternal grandmother’s now solicitor, and I am grateful for his attendance today. I appreciate he has only very recently been retained in the matter.
Ultimately, the Department decided not to advance the matter any further, where the paternal grandmother could keep an eye on X when he was there, and that the child was now living with the maternal grandmother and she had acted protectively by taking the child to an emergency department for examination.
Again, the last part of that information [the hospital visit] did not find its way into the maternal grandmother’s affidavit.

Notice to applicant

When the Court reviewed the co-located material this morning, I had my chambers contact the maternal grandmother in the following terms:
Dear [Ms Melton],

We refer to the mention before the Honourable Brasch at 2.00pm (AEST/Qld time) / 3.00pm (ADST/NSW time) today, 5 March 2026.

I have re-attached the Microsoft Teams link below for your convenience.

Prior to the hearing, Her Honour has asked chambers to provide you with a copy of the attached draft proforma orders which Her Honour often makes in matters such as these on the first return.

Please also note that chambers has received information received from the Department of Communities and Justice (NSW) that indicated the child’s father is [Mr Edwards] and the paternal grandmother is [Ms F] (last name redacted). Her Honour will ask you to address this at the hearing this afternoon and the circumstances of the alleged sexual abuse by a cousin at [Ms F’s] home, none of which the applicant deposes to.

(Emphasis in original)
It may be that after that, Mr Mba was engaged by the maternal grandmother, and I am grateful for his presence this afternoon. He will be filing a Notice of Address for Service in due course.

The importance of information sharing

This is a matter that highlights the critical importance of the information sharing regime that now finds its way into the Act.
Had I not had that information, in particular Exhibit 2, I would have been none the wiser that the child in fact has a father, who, under the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth), is a necessary party.[5] I will be making Orders for [him to be a party and for] these proceedings to be brought to his attention.
I also would have had no idea about the allegations that have been made with respect to X and his six-year-old cousin. I make no findings about that at all today, but it is important to note that the maternal grandmother acted protectively in taking the child to the emergency department. Ultimately, the Department have closed their file and are not advancing the matter for the reasons I have already stated.
The Court would have been none the wiser to any of that without the important information sharing provisions that the Act now contains.

Next steps

I will make an Order that a copy of these Reasons be provided to the specific co-located officer from the Department of Community Justice (NSW) for placing on the child’s file.
I will also make a further Order that when these Reasons are de-identified, the Principal Registrar of this Court, or his delegate, provide a copy of the de-identified Reasons to all co‑located officers so they can appreciate the important work they do and the importance of information sharing.
I now have a better picture of the child and am therefore comfortable that the Orders I discussed with Mr Mba are, on this first return, in the child’s best interests and that the procedural orders are appropriate.
I pause to add that after I took Mr Mba through Exhibit 2 from the Department, I stood down so he could have an opportunity to speak with his client and take instructions from her on those matters.
Accordingly, I will make the Orders as discussed with Mr Mba to which his client agreed.
